10: # Code Workflows - Surface-Aware Smart Router
11: 
12: `sk-code` is the single code-work skill. It first decides which code surface is in front of it, then loads the right implementation, quality, debugging, and verification resources.
13: 
14: **Core principle**: `Code surface detection -> Intent classification -> Surface resources -> Verification evidence`.
15: 
16: ---
17: 
18: ## 1. WHEN TO USE
19: 
20: Use this skill when doing code work in either supported surface:
21: 
22: - **WEBFLOW**: Webflow / vanilla frontend work in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, including Motion.dev runtime usage, GSAP, Lenis, HLS, Swiper, FilePond, CDN/minification, and browser verification.
23: - **OPENCODE**: OpenCode system work under `.opencode/`, including skills, agents, commands, MCP servers, hooks, scripts, tests, JSON/JSONC config, TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, and Shell.
24: 
25: Also use this skill for cross-stack Motion.dev reference work when the question is about Motion APIs, snippets, integration modes, performance pitfalls, or CSS/Motion/GSAP/WAAPI trade-offs that should live in `references/motion_dev/` or `assets/motion_dev/` rather than inside Webflow-only guidance.
26: 
27: Use it for implementation, code quality, debugging, verification, test failures, build failures, and before any completion claim.
28: 
29: Do **not** use this skill for documentation-only changes (`sk-doc`), git workflow (`sk-git`), pure browser inspection (`mcp-chrome-devtools`), or formal findings-first review output (`sk-code-review` baseline plus this skill's surface evidence).
30: 
31: Documentation-only edits to skill markdown route to `sk-doc`, even when the file lives under `.opencode/skills/`. Examples: updating a `SKILL.md` headline, clarifying a README paragraph, rewriting a description section, or adding a one-line summary at the top of a markdown file. Negative example: "Update the sk-code SKILL.md headline section to clarify the two-axis routing model and add a one-line summary" is `sk-doc`, not `sk-code`, because the requested change is prose-only and does not modify executable behavior or routing logic.
32: 
33: ### Phase Overview
34: 
35: | Phase | Purpose | Requirement |
36: | --- | --- | --- |
37: | Phase 0: Research | Understand unfamiliar code or risky changes | Optional, but required for complex work |
38: | Phase 1: Implementation | Write or modify code using surface patterns | Read actual files first; apply the Design Restraint Ladder before writing new code. |
39: | Phase 1.5: Code Quality Gate | Apply P0/P1/P2 checks and surface standards | Required before claiming implementation done |
40: | Phase 2: Debugging | Trace symptom to root cause and fix one cause at a time | Required when tests/runtime fail |
41: | Phase 3: Verification | Run surface verification commands and record evidence | Required before any done/works claim |
42: 
43: **Iron Law**: no completion claim without fresh verification evidence from the detected surface.
44: 
45: **Baseline & blast-radius**: before Phase 1, capture the starting gate state (pass/fail counts + the names of failing tests, base commit) so Phase 3 reports the *delta*, not just a green — a green suite says nothing about a path it never ran. Open non-trivial work with a one-phrase blast-radius read ("low-blast, reversible" / "high-blast: touches auth + data") so effort matches stakes.
46: 
47: ### Review Baseline Contract
48: 
49: `sk-code-review` owns findings format, severity model, and baseline security/quality/test review. `sk-code` owns surface detection and surface-specific standards evidence.
50: 
51: ### Cross-Skill Consumption
52: 
53: When called from `/speckit:complete` with an `.opencode/` implementation target (`step_10_development` activity), `sk-code` surfaces the matching authoring checklist plus the `spec_folder_write` recipe AT WRITE-TIME (before the orchestrator's first write), not just at review-time.
54: 
55: | Target Path | Authoring Checklist Surfaced | Recipe |
56: |---|---|---|
57: | `.opencode/skills/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/skill_authoring.md` | — |
58: | `.opencode/agents/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/agent_authoring.md` | — |
59: | `.opencode/commands/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/command_authoring.md` | — |
60: | `.opencode/specs/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/spec_folder_authoring.md` | `assets/opencode/recipes/spec_folder_write.md` |
61: | MCP server source | `assets/opencode/checklists/mcp_server_authoring.md` | — |
62: 
63: Authoring-time load is the contract documented in `system-spec-kit/SKILL.md §16-17 cross-skill routing` and the `cross_skill_authoring_load` block in `/speckit:complete` YAMLs. Review-time `sk-code-review` baseline + `sk-code` router-selected evidence overlay remains unchanged.
64: 
65: ---
66: 
67: ## 2. SMART ROUTING
68: 
69: ### Surface Detection (FIRST)
70: 
71: Detection is context-aware and uses CWD plus changed/target files. **Precedence**: OPENCODE target/CWD wins over WEBFLOW markers (because mixed-marker workspaces are common — `.opencode/` system tools sometimes ship frontend animation libraries internally). When neither matches, fall through to UNKNOWN.
72: 
73: Machine-readable stack folder contract:
74: 
75: ```python
76: STACK_FOLDERS = {
77:     "WEBFLOW": ["src/2_javascript/", "*.webflow.js"],
78:     "OPENCODE": [".opencode/skills/", ".opencode/agents/", ".opencode/commands/", ".opencode/specs/"],
79:     "MOTION_DEV": ["references/motion_dev/", "assets/motion_dev/"],
80: }
81: ```
82: 
83: ```bash
84: # Use early-return precedence — never let later branches overwrite an earlier match.
85: 
86: # 1. OPENCODE - takes precedence: CWD or any changed/target file under .opencode/
87: if [[ "$PWD" == */.opencode/* ]] \
88:    || [[ "$TARGET_FILE" == */.opencode/* ]]; then
89:   SURFACE="OPENCODE"
90: 
91: # 2. Explicit non-Webflow guard - a prompt can ask for Motion.dev cross-stack
92: # guidance without making the implementation surface WEBFLOW.
93: elif printf '%s\n' "${PROMPT_TEXT:-}" | grep -Eiq \
94:      '(^|[^[:alnum:]])(not webflow|no webflow designer|without webflow|non-webflow|vanilla html/css/js only|vanilla html css js only|stack-agnostic)([^[:alnum:]]|$)'; then
95:   SURFACE="UNKNOWN"
96: 
97: # 3. WEBFLOW - frontend HTML/CSS/JS and Webflow-specific vanilla animation web
98: elif [[ -d "src/2_javascript" ]] \
99:      || ls *.webflow.js 2>/dev/null | head -1 \
100:      || grep -lq "Webflow\.push\|--vw-" src/**/*.{js,css,html} 2>/dev/null \
101:      || grep -lqE "window\.Motion|window\.gsap|gsap\.(to|from|set|timeline|registerPlugin)|new Lenis|new Hls|new Swiper|FilePond" \
102:         src/**/*.{js,mjs,ts,html} *.{js,mjs,ts,html} 2>/dev/null \
103:      || [[ -f "wrangler.toml" ]]; then
104:   SURFACE="WEBFLOW"
105: 
106: # 4. UNKNOWN - not owned by this skill; ask for runtime + verification commands
107: else
108:   SURFACE="UNKNOWN"
109: fi
110: ```
111: 
112: **Why OPENCODE wins precedence**: `.opencode/skills/sk-doc/scripts/preview-server.js` is an OPENCODE system tool that may import vanilla animation libraries (Lenis, GSAP) for its preview UI. A first-match-WEBFLOW pseudocode would route this OPENCODE work to the wrong standards. The target/CWD path is the strongest unambiguous signal of which surface owns the work.
113: 
114: **Supported surfaces**:
115: 
116: - `WEBFLOW`: frontend HTML/CSS/JS, Webflow conventions, vanilla animation libraries, CDN/minification, and browser evidence.
117: - `OPENCODE`: `.opencode/` system code and config with language sub-detection.
118: - `UNKNOWN`: ask a short disambiguation question and do not pretend unsupported stacks are covered.
119: 
120: For details: `references/stack_detection.md`.
121: 
122: ### UNKNOWN_FALLBACK Checklist
123: 
124: Returned when no supported surface matches, when intent confidence is low (`max(intent_scores) < 0.5`), or when the user explicitly asks for stack-agnostic guidance. Ask for the missing routing inputs instead of guessing:
125: 
126: - Confirm the active runtime surface — Webflow/frontend or `.opencode/` system code.
127: - Confirm the task intent (implementation / debugging / verification / etc.).
128: - Provide one concrete input, error, or expected output.
129: - Confirm the verification command set before any completion claim.
130: 
131: Do not load Go / Next.js / React Native / Swift resources — canonical `sk-code` owns only WEBFLOW + OPENCODE + MOTION_DEV. Full fallback logic: `references/smart_routing.md §8`.
132: 
133: ### Phase Detection
134: 
135: ```text
136: TASK CONTEXT
137:     |
138:     +- STEP 0: Detect surface from CWD + target files (primary routing key)
139:     |    +- references/<surface>/  (webflow / opencode)
140:     |    +- assets/<surface>/      (webflow / opencode)
141:     |
142:     +- STEP 1: Detect language sub-key (OPENCODE only) for verification commands
143:     |
144:     +- STEP 2: Weighted intent scoring (top-2 when ambiguity delta is small)
145:     |
146:     +- Phase 1: Implementation -> per-language style + standards + implementation trio
147:     +- Phase 2: Debugging       -> debugging refs + universal error recovery
148:     +- Phase 3: Verification    -> surface-appropriate verification commands + checklist
149: ```
150: 
151: **The Iron Law**: NO COMPLETION CLAIMS WITHOUT RUNNING SURFACE-APPROPRIATE VERIFICATION.
152: 
153: Phase contract details: [`references/phase_detection.md`](./references/phase_detection.md).
154: 
155: ### OPENCODE Language Sub-Detection
156: 
157: When surface is `OPENCODE`, detect language from changed/target file extensions first, then weighted keywords:
158: 
159: | Language | Extensions / Signals | Resources |
160: | --- | --- | --- |
161: | JAVASCRIPT | `.js`, `.mjs`, `.cjs`, CommonJS, Node, MCP | `references/opencode/javascript/*` |
162: | TYPESCRIPT | `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.mts`, `.d.ts`, tsconfig, interfaces | `references/opencode/typescript/*` |
163: | PYTHON | `.py`, pytest, argparse, docstrings | `references/opencode/python/*` |
164: | SHELL | `.sh`, `.bash`, shebang, pipefail | `references/opencode/shell/*` |
165: | CONFIG | `.json`, `.jsonc`, schema, descriptor | `references/opencode/config/*` |
166: 
167: Ambiguous multi-language tasks load the top matching language references plus the universal OpenCode checklist.
168: 
169: ### Resource Domains
170: 
171: - `references/universal/`: surface-agnostic error recovery, code quality, style, and research guidance.
172: - `references/`: detection, intent scoring, loading, and lifecycle internals.
173: - `references/webflow/`, `assets/webflow/`: live Webflow/frontend per-language references under `references/webflow/{javascript,css,html}/*` — JS and CSS each carry `style_guide.md`, `quality_standards.md`, `quick_reference.md`; CSS additionally carries `patterns.md` (Webflow tokens, state machines, focus/form patterns); HTML carries `style_guide.md` only (Webflow Designer manages most HTML). Cross-language rules + enforcement workflow + dev workflow live under `references/webflow/shared/*`. Categorical workflow patterns (implementation, debugging, verification, performance, deployment) and copy-paste templates (`assets/webflow/templates/component_template.{js,css}`) round out the surface. Mirrors the OPENCODE per-language layout so the smart router resolves both surfaces with identical key-derived patterns.
174: - `references/motion_dev/`, `assets/motion_dev/`: cross-stack Motion.dev API, timeline, scroll/gesture, performance, decision-matrix, integration, install, playbook hook, and snippet resources. Webflow docs link here for generic Motion details while keeping Webflow-CDN and Designer guidance in `references/webflow/`.
175: - `references/opencode/`, `assets/opencode/`: OpenCode system-code language standards, shared patterns, hooks, alignment automation, and quality checklists.
176: - `assets/webflow/scripts/`: Webflow build, minification, and runtime verification utilities.
177: - `assets/scripts/`: Cross-surface helper scripts, including the OpenCode alignment verifier.
178: 
179: ### Resource Loading Levels
180: 
181: Loading follows the canonical three levels; the finer `ALWAYS / SURFACE / INTENT / LANGUAGE / ON_DEMAND` tiers live in `references/smart_routing.md §3`.
182: 
183: | Level | When to Load | Resources |
184: | --- | --- | --- |
185: | ALWAYS | Every sk-code invocation | `references/stack_detection.md`, `references/smart_routing.md`, `references/phase_detection.md`, and the `references/universal/` quality + error-recovery baseline |
186: | CONDITIONAL | After surface + intent (and OPENCODE language) detection | the detected `references/<surface>/` + `assets/<surface>/` trees, the matching language standards, intent-mapped resources, the authoring checklists below, and `references/motion_dev/` for `MOTION_DEV` intent |
187: | ON_DEMAND | Only on an explicit deep-dive request | extended checklists and niche references, plus the full `INTENT_MODEL` / `RESOURCE_MAP` in `references/smart_routing.md` |
188: 
189: ### OpenCode Authoring Resources
190: 
191: | Resource | Path | When to load |
192: |---|---|---|
193: | skill_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/skill_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring new skill) |
194: | agent_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/agent_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring new agent) |
195: | command_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/command_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring new command) |
196: | mcp_server_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/mcp_server_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring MCP server) |
197: | spec_folder_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/spec_folder_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: spec folder write) |
198: | spec_folder_write recipe | `assets/opencode/recipes/spec_folder_write.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: spec folder write) |
199: 
200: ### Intent Classification
201: 
202: After surface detection, score task text for intents: `IMPLEMENTATION`, `CODE_QUALITY`, `DEBUGGING`, `VERIFICATION`, `TESTING`, `DEPLOYMENT`, `PERFORMANCE`, `ANIMATION`, `MOTION_DEV`, `FORMS`, `VIDEO`, `API`, `HOOKS`, `CONFIG`, and `LANGUAGE_STANDARDS`.
203: 
204: Top intent always loads. A close second intent also loads when scores are within the ambiguity threshold.
205: 
206: `MOTION_DEV` is a resource intent, not a third code surface. It loads `references/motion_dev/` and `assets/motion_dev/` for cross-stack Motion questions after WEBFLOW/OPENCODE/UNKNOWN surface handling has established where implementation work is happening.
207: 
208: ### Verification Commands
209: 
210: | Surface | Required verification evidence |
211: | --- | --- |
212: | WEBFLOW | `node .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/webflow/scripts/minify-webflow.mjs`, `node .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/webflow/scripts/verify-minification.mjs`, `node .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/webflow/scripts/test-minified-runtime.mjs`, plus desktop/mobile browser console clean evidence when runtime behavior changes |
213: | OPENCODE | `python3 .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/scripts/verify_alignment_drift.py --root <changed-scope>`, plus targeted language/project tests such as vitest, pytest, shellcheck, JSON validation, or spec validation for changed spec folders |
214: | UNKNOWN | User-selected verification command set before completion claim |
215: 
216: ### Verification Rituals
217: 
218: Apply these alongside the commands above, scaled to blast radius:
219: 
220: - **Mutation check / claim-falsifier (after green).** A passing test proves nothing until you have seen it fail for the right reason. After green, break the production code the test guards, confirm that specific test fails, then restore. Distinguish **true-RED** (the assertion fails against correct intent) from **compile-RED** (the suite never compiled or ran — not a satisfying RED). A test that stays green when you break the thing it guards is a vacuous test — a defect, not coverage.

# Code Workflows - Surface-Aware Smart Router

`sk-code` is the single code-work skill. It first decides which code surface is in front of it, then loads the right implementation, quality, debugging, and verification resources.

**Core principle**: `Code surface detection -> Intent classification -> Surface resources -> Verification evidence`.

---

## 1. WHEN TO USE

Use this skill when doing code work in either supported surface:

- **WEBFLOW**: Webflow / vanilla frontend work in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, including Motion.dev runtime usage, GSAP, Lenis, HLS, Swiper, FilePond, CDN/minification, and browser verification.
- **OPENCODE**: OpenCode system work under `.opencode/`, including skills, agents, commands, MCP servers, hooks, scripts, tests, JSON/JSONC config, TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, and Shell.

Also use this skill for cross-stack Motion.dev reference work when the question is about Motion APIs, snippets, integration modes, performance pitfalls, or CSS/Motion/GSAP/WAAPI trade-offs that should live in `references/motion_dev/` or `assets/motion_dev/` rather than inside Webflow-only guidance.

Use it for implementation, code quality, debugging, verification, test failures, build failures, and before any completion claim.

Do **not** use this skill for documentation-only changes (`sk-doc`), git workflow (`sk-git`), pure browser inspection (`mcp-chrome-devtools`), or formal findings-first review output (`sk-code-review` baseline plus this skill's surface evidence).

Documentation-only edits to skill markdown route to `sk-doc`, even when the file lives under `.opencode/skills/`. Examples: updating a `SKILL.md` headline, clarifying a README paragraph, rewriting a description section, or adding a one-line summary at the top of a markdown file. Negative example: "Update the sk-code SKILL.md headline section to clarify the two-axis routing model and add a one-line summary" is `sk-doc`, not `sk-code`, because the requested change is prose-only and does not modify executable behavior or routing logic.

### Phase Overview

| Phase | Purpose | Requirement |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Phase 0: Research | Understand unfamiliar code or risky changes | Optional, but required for complex work |
| Phase 1: Implementation | Write or modify code using surface patterns | Read actual files first; apply the Design Restraint Ladder before writing new code. |
| Phase 1.5: Code Quality Gate | Apply P0/P1/P2 checks and surface standards | Required before claiming implementation done |
| Phase 2: Debugging | Trace symptom to root cause and fix one cause at a time | Required when tests/runtime fail |
| Phase 3: Verification | Run surface verification commands and record evidence | Required before any done/works claim |

**Iron Law**: no completion claim without fresh verification evidence from the detected surface.

**Baseline & blast-radius**: before Phase 1, capture the starting gate state (pass/fail counts + the names of failing tests, base commit) so Phase 3 reports the *delta*, not just a green — a green suite says nothing about a path it never ran. Open non-trivial work with a one-phrase blast-radius read ("low-blast, reversible" / "high-blast: touches auth + data") so effort matches stakes.

### Review Baseline Contract

`sk-code-review` owns findings format, severity model, and baseline security/quality/test review. `sk-code` owns surface detection and surface-specific standards evidence.

### Cross-Skill Consumption

When called from `/speckit:complete` with an `.opencode/` implementation target (`step_10_development` activity), `sk-code` surfaces the matching authoring checklist plus the `spec_folder_write` recipe AT WRITE-TIME (before the orchestrator's first write), not just at review-time.

| Target Path | Authoring Checklist Surfaced | Recipe |
|---|---|---|
| `.opencode/skills/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/skill_authoring.md` | — |
| `.opencode/agents/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/agent_authoring.md` | — |
| `.opencode/commands/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/command_authoring.md` | — |
| `.opencode/specs/` | `assets/opencode/checklists/spec_folder_authoring.md` | `assets/opencode/recipes/spec_folder_write.md` |
| MCP server source | `assets/opencode/checklists/mcp_server_authoring.md` | — |

Authoring-time load is the contract documented in `system-spec-kit/SKILL.md §16-17 cross-skill routing` and the `cross_skill_authoring_load` block in `/speckit:complete` YAMLs. Review-time `sk-code-review` baseline + `sk-code` router-selected evidence overlay remains unchanged.

---

## 2. SMART ROUTING

### Surface Detection (FIRST)

Detection is context-aware and uses CWD plus changed/target files. **Precedence**: OPENCODE target/CWD wins over WEBFLOW markers (because mixed-marker workspaces are common — `.opencode/` system tools sometimes ship frontend animation libraries internally). When neither matches, fall through to UNKNOWN.

Machine-readable stack folder contract:

```python
STACK_FOLDERS = {
    "WEBFLOW": ["src/2_javascript/", "*.webflow.js"],
    "OPENCODE": [".opencode/skills/", ".opencode/agents/", ".opencode/commands/", ".opencode/specs/"],
    "MOTION_DEV": ["references/motion_dev/", "assets/motion_dev/"],
}
```

```bash
# Use early-return precedence — never let later branches overwrite an earlier match.

# 1. OPENCODE - takes precedence: CWD or any changed/target file under .opencode/
if [[ "$PWD" == */.opencode/* ]] \
   || [[ "$TARGET_FILE" == */.opencode/* ]]; then
  SURFACE="OPENCODE"

# 2. Explicit non-Webflow guard - a prompt can ask for Motion.dev cross-stack
# guidance without making the implementation surface WEBFLOW.
elif printf '%s\n' "${PROMPT_TEXT:-}" | grep -Eiq \
     '(^|[^[:alnum:]])(not webflow|no webflow designer|without webflow|non-webflow|vanilla html/css/js only|vanilla html css js only|stack-agnostic)([^[:alnum:]]|$)'; then
  SURFACE="UNKNOWN"

# 3. WEBFLOW - frontend HTML/CSS/JS and Webflow-specific vanilla animation web
elif [[ -d "src/2_javascript" ]] \
     || ls *.webflow.js 2>/dev/null | head -1 \
     || grep -lq "Webflow\.push\|--vw-" src/**/*.{js,css,html} 2>/dev/null \
     || grep -lqE "window\.Motion|window\.gsap|gsap\.(to|from|set|timeline|registerPlugin)|new Lenis|new Hls|new Swiper|FilePond" \
        src/**/*.{js,mjs,ts,html} *.{js,mjs,ts,html} 2>/dev/null \
     || [[ -f "wrangler.toml" ]]; then
  SURFACE="WEBFLOW"

# 4. UNKNOWN - not owned by this skill; ask for runtime + verification commands
else
  SURFACE="UNKNOWN"
fi
```

**Why OPENCODE wins precedence**: `.opencode/skills/sk-doc/scripts/preview-server.js` is an OPENCODE system tool that may import vanilla animation libraries (Lenis, GSAP) for its preview UI. A first-match-WEBFLOW pseudocode would route this OPENCODE work to the wrong standards. The target/CWD path is the strongest unambiguous signal of which surface owns the work.

**Supported surfaces**:

- `WEBFLOW`: frontend HTML/CSS/JS, Webflow conventions, vanilla animation libraries, CDN/minification, and browser evidence.
- `OPENCODE`: `.opencode/` system code and config with language sub-detection.
- `UNKNOWN`: ask a short disambiguation question and do not pretend unsupported stacks are covered.

For details: `references/stack_detection.md`.

### UNKNOWN_FALLBACK Checklist

Returned when no supported surface matches, when intent confidence is low (`max(intent_scores) < 0.5`), or when the user explicitly asks for stack-agnostic guidance. Ask for the missing routing inputs instead of guessing:

- Confirm the active runtime surface — Webflow/frontend or `.opencode/` system code.
- Confirm the task intent (implementation / debugging / verification / etc.).
- Provide one concrete input, error, or expected output.
- Confirm the verification command set before any completion claim.

Do not load Go / Next.js / React Native / Swift resources — canonical `sk-code` owns only WEBFLOW + OPENCODE + MOTION_DEV. Full fallback logic: `references/smart_routing.md §8`.

### Phase Detection

```text
TASK CONTEXT
    |
    +- STEP 0: Detect surface from CWD + target files (primary routing key)
    |    +- references/<surface>/  (webflow / opencode)
    |    +- assets/<surface>/      (webflow / opencode)
    |
    +- STEP 1: Detect language sub-key (OPENCODE only) for verification commands
    |
    +- STEP 2: Weighted intent scoring (top-2 when ambiguity delta is small)
    |
    +- Phase 1: Implementation -> per-language style + standards + implementation trio
    +- Phase 2: Debugging       -> debugging refs + universal error recovery
    +- Phase 3: Verification    -> surface-appropriate verification commands + checklist
```

**The Iron Law**: NO COMPLETION CLAIMS WITHOUT RUNNING SURFACE-APPROPRIATE VERIFICATION.

Phase contract details: [`references/phase_detection.md`](./references/phase_detection.md).

### OPENCODE Language Sub-Detection

When surface is `OPENCODE`, detect language from changed/target file extensions first, then weighted keywords:

| Language | Extensions / Signals | Resources |
| --- | --- | --- |
| JAVASCRIPT | `.js`, `.mjs`, `.cjs`, CommonJS, Node, MCP | `references/opencode/javascript/*` |
| TYPESCRIPT | `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.mts`, `.d.ts`, tsconfig, interfaces | `references/opencode/typescript/*` |
| PYTHON | `.py`, pytest, argparse, docstrings | `references/opencode/python/*` |
| SHELL | `.sh`, `.bash`, shebang, pipefail | `references/opencode/shell/*` |
| CONFIG | `.json`, `.jsonc`, schema, descriptor | `references/opencode/config/*` |

Ambiguous multi-language tasks load the top matching language references plus the universal OpenCode checklist.

### Resource Domains

- `references/universal/`: surface-agnostic error recovery, code quality, style, and research guidance.
- `references/`: detection, intent scoring, loading, and lifecycle internals.
- `references/webflow/`, `assets/webflow/`: live Webflow/frontend per-language references under `references/webflow/{javascript,css,html}/*` — JS and CSS each carry `style_guide.md`, `quality_standards.md`, `quick_reference.md`; CSS additionally carries `patterns.md` (Webflow tokens, state machines, focus/form patterns); HTML carries `style_guide.md` only (Webflow Designer manages most HTML). Cross-language rules + enforcement workflow + dev workflow live under `references/webflow/shared/*`. Categorical workflow patterns (implementation, debugging, verification, performance, deployment) and copy-paste templates (`assets/webflow/templates/component_template.{js,css}`) round out the surface. Mirrors the OPENCODE per-language layout so the smart router resolves both surfaces with identical key-derived patterns.
- `references/motion_dev/`, `assets/motion_dev/`: cross-stack Motion.dev API, timeline, scroll/gesture, performance, decision-matrix, integration, install, playbook hook, and snippet resources. Webflow docs link here for generic Motion details while keeping Webflow-CDN and Designer guidance in `references/webflow/`.
- `references/opencode/`, `assets/opencode/`: OpenCode system-code language standards, shared patterns, hooks, alignment automation, and quality checklists.
- `assets/webflow/scripts/`: Webflow build, minification, and runtime verification utilities.
- `assets/scripts/`: Cross-surface helper scripts, including the OpenCode alignment verifier.

### Resource Loading Levels

Loading follows the canonical three levels; the finer `ALWAYS / SURFACE / INTENT / LANGUAGE / ON_DEMAND` tiers live in `references/smart_routing.md §3`.

| Level | When to Load | Resources |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ALWAYS | Every sk-code invocation | `references/stack_detection.md`, `references/smart_routing.md`, `references/phase_detection.md`, and the `references/universal/` quality + error-recovery baseline |
| CONDITIONAL | After surface + intent (and OPENCODE language) detection | the detected `references/<surface>/` + `assets/<surface>/` trees, the matching language standards, intent-mapped resources, the authoring checklists below, and `references/motion_dev/` for `MOTION_DEV` intent |
| ON_DEMAND | Only on an explicit deep-dive request | extended checklists and niche references, plus the full `INTENT_MODEL` / `RESOURCE_MAP` in `references/smart_routing.md` |

### OpenCode Authoring Resources

| Resource | Path | When to load |
|---|---|---|
| skill_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/skill_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring new skill) |
| agent_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/agent_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring new agent) |
| command_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/command_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring new command) |
| mcp_server_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/mcp_server_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: authoring MCP server) |
| spec_folder_authoring | `assets/opencode/checklists/spec_folder_authoring.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: spec folder write) |
| spec_folder_write recipe | `assets/opencode/recipes/spec_folder_write.md` | CONDITIONAL (intent: spec folder write) |

### Intent Classification

After surface detection, score task text for intents: `IMPLEMENTATION`, `CODE_QUALITY`, `DEBUGGING`, `VERIFICATION`, `TESTING`, `DEPLOYMENT`, `PERFORMANCE`, `ANIMATION`, `MOTION_DEV`, `FORMS`, `VIDEO`, `API`, `HOOKS`, `CONFIG`, and `LANGUAGE_STANDARDS`.

Top intent always loads. A close second intent also loads when scores are within the ambiguity threshold.

`MOTION_DEV` is a resource intent, not a third code surface. It loads `references/motion_dev/` and `assets/motion_dev/` for cross-stack Motion questions after WEBFLOW/OPENCODE/UNKNOWN surface handling has established where implementation work is happening.

### Verification Commands

| Surface | Required verification evidence |
| --- | --- |
| WEBFLOW | `node .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/webflow/scripts/minify-webflow.mjs`, `node .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/webflow/scripts/verify-minification.mjs`, `node .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/webflow/scripts/test-minified-runtime.mjs`, plus desktop/mobile browser console clean evidence when runtime behavior changes |
| OPENCODE | `python3 .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/scripts/verify_alignment_drift.py --root <changed-scope>`, plus targeted language/project tests such as vitest, pytest, shellcheck, JSON validation, or spec validation for changed spec folders |
| UNKNOWN | User-selected verification command set before completion claim |

### Verification Rituals

Apply these alongside the commands above, scaled to blast radius:

- **Mutation check / claim-falsifier (after green).** A passing test proves nothing until you have seen it fail for the right reason. After green, break the production code the test guards, confirm that specific test fails, then restore. Distinguish **true-RED** (the assertion fails against correct intent) from **compile-RED** (the suite never compiled or ran — not a satisfying RED). A test that stays green when you break the thing it guards is a vacuous test — a defect, not coverage.

  - check-comment-hygiene
  - comment hygiene
  - ephemeral artifact
  - ephemeral pointer
---

# Comment Hygiene — No Ephemeral Artifact Pointers in Code Comments

> Always-surface constitutional rule. Enforcement at pre-commit gate and Claude Code PostToolUse hook.

## The Rule

Never embed spec-folder paths, packet/phase numbers, ADR ids, task/checklist/requirement ids, or finding ids in code comments. These labels are perishable — they rot as specs are renamed, renumbered, consolidated, or archived.

Keep the durable WHY (the reason the code exists or behaves a certain way). Drop the perishable label (the ephemeral identifier that points to it).

## Forbidden vs. Allowed

| Category | Forbidden (perishable) | Allowed (durable) |
| -------- | ---------------------- | ----------------- |
| Spec paths | `.opencode/specs/012-foo/` | — |
| Packet/phase numbers | `see packet 031`, `phase 004` | — |
| ADR identifiers | `ADR-017`, `per ADR-003` | full rationale inline |
| Requirement ids | `REQ-042`, `CHK-007` | plain-language description |
| Task/checklist ids | `T042`, `checklist item 3` | — |
| Finding ids | `P1-finding-2`, `finding #7` | — |
| Standard body refs | — | `CWE-89`, `RFC 7230`, `POSIX 2017`, `WEBFLOW:`, `ISO 8601` |

Standard body identifiers (CWE, RFC, POSIX, platform tags) are stable and durable — use them freely.

## Code Example

```python
# BAD — ephemeral pointer that rots
# Fix per ADR-017 and REQ-042 (spec: .opencode/specs/031-embedding-stack/004-wal/)
conn.execute("PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)")

# GOOD — durable WHY
# Truncate WAL on close to prevent unbounded growth on long-idle connections.
conn.execute("PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)")
```

## Enforcement

Three gates check every code comment write:

1. **Pre-commit gate** — `check-comment-hygiene.sh` runs as a pre-commit hook and fails the commit if any staged file contains forbidden patterns in comments.
2. **Claude Code PostToolUse hook** — fires after every Edit/Write tool call and flags violations inline before the response is returned.
3. **CI gate** — `.github/workflows/comment-hygiene.yml` re-validates on every PR to main; this gate cannot be bypassed with `--no-verify`.

The pre-commit gate cannot be bypassed with `--no-verify` but can be skipped with `SPECKIT_SKIP_COMMENT_HYGIENE=1` (requires explicit operator intent). The CI gate cannot be bypassed from the command line. Individual lines may be exempted by appending `// hygiene-ok` — use only for documented false-positives (e.g. stable standard-body refs that trigger a pattern).

*Constitutional Memory — Always surfaces at top of search results*

1: ---
2: title: Shell Style Guide
3: description: Coding conventions and formatting standards for Bash scripts in the OpenCode development environment.
4: trigger_phrases:
5:   - "opencode shell style guide"
6:   - "bash formatting standards"
7:   - "shell shebang header"
8:   - "bash naming conventions"
9: importance_tier: normal
10: contextType: implementation
11: ---
12: 
13: # Shell Style Guide
14: 
15: Coding conventions and formatting standards for Bash scripts in the OpenCode development environment.
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 1. OVERVIEW
20: 
21: ### Purpose
22: 
23: Defines consistent styling rules for Bash scripts to ensure reliability, readability, and maintainability across all OpenCode shell scripts.
24: 
25: ### Scope
26: 
27: Applies to all shell files in:
28: - `.opencode/skills/*/scripts/` - Skill automation scripts
29: - `.opencode/agents/scripts/` - Agent provider scripts
30: - `scripts/` - Build and deployment scripts
31: 
32: ### Key Sources
33: 
34: | File | Evidence |
35: |------|----------|
36: | `lib/common.sh` | Color definitions, logging functions, utilities |
37: | `spec/create.sh` | Strict mode, argument parsing, control flow |
38: 
39: ---
40: 
41: ## 2. FILE STRUCTURE
42: 
43: ### Shebang Line
44: 
45: **ALWAYS** use the portable bash shebang:
46: 
47: ```bash
48: #!/usr/bin/env bash
49: ```
50: 
51: **Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:1`
52: 
53: ### File Header
54: 
55: Use the standard header format for identification:
56: 
57: ```bash
58: #!/usr/bin/env bash
59: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
60: # COMPONENT: [COMPONENT NAME]
61: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
62: # Brief description of what this script does.
63: #
64: # Usage: script.sh [options] <arguments>
65: ```
66: 
67: **Alternative** (detailed header for major scripts):
68: 
69: ```bash
70: #!/usr/bin/env bash
71: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72: # SPECKIT: CREATE SPEC FOLDER
73: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
74: # Creates spec folder with templates based on documentation level.
75: #
76: # TEMPLATE ARCHITECTURE (v2.0 - CORE + ADDENDUM):
77: #   templates/
78: #   ├── level_1/        # Core only (~270 LOC total)
79: #   ├── level_2/        # Core + Verification (~390 LOC)
80: #   └── level_3/        # Core + Verification + Architecture (~540 LOC)
81: ```
82: 
83: **Evidence**: `spec/create.sh:1-20`
84: 
85: ### Strict Mode
86: 
87: **ALWAYS** enable strict mode immediately after the shebang/header:
88: 
89: ```bash
90: set -euo pipefail
91: ```
92: 
93: | Flag | Purpose |
94: |------|---------|
95: | `-e` | Exit on error (non-zero return) |
96: | `-u` | Error on undefined variables |
97: | `-o pipefail` | Pipe fails if any command fails |
98: 
99: **Evidence**: `spec/create.sh:22`
100: 
101: ### Section Organization
102: 
103: Organize code into numbered sections:
104: 
105: ```bash
106: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
107: # 1. COLOR DEFINITIONS
108: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
109: 
110: RED='\033[0;31m'
111: GREEN='\033[0;32m'
112: NC='\033[0m'
113: 
114: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
115: # 2. UTILITY FUNCTIONS
116: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
117: 
118: log_info() {
119:     printf "${BLUE}INFO:${NC} %s\n" "$1"
120: }
121: ```
122: 
123: **Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:9-35`
124: 
125: ---
126: 
127: ## 3. NAMING CONVENTIONS
128: 
129: ### Functions
130: 
131: **Use**: `lowercase_with_underscores`
132: 
133: ```bash
134: # Public functions
135: validate_input() {
136:     local input="$1"
137:     # ...
138: }
139: 
140: process_file() {
141:     local file_path="$1"
142:     # ...
143: }
144: ```
145: 
146: ### Private/Internal Functions
147: 
148: **Use**: Single underscore prefix for helpers:
149: 
150: ```bash
151: # Internal helper function
152: _json_escape() {
153:     local str="$1"
154:     str="${str//\\/\\\\}"
155:     str="${str//\"/\\\"}"
156:     printf '%s' "$str"
157: }
158: ```
159: 
160: **Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:37-45`
161: 
162: ### Variables
163: 
164: **Use**: `lowercase_with_underscores` for local variables:
165: 
166: ```bash
167: local file_path="$1"
168: local output_dir="${2:-./output}"
169: local is_valid=true
170: ```
171: 
172: ### Constants and Global Variables
173: 
174: **Use**: `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for constants and script-level variables:
175: 
176: ```bash
177: readonly MAX_RETRIES=3
178: readonly DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=30
179: 
180: # Script-level state
181: ERRORS=0
182: WARNINGS=0
183: JSON_MODE=false
184: ```
185: 
186: **Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:28-31`, `spec/create.sh:24-33`
187: 
188: ---
189: 
190: ## 4. COLOR DEFINITIONS
191: 
192: ### Standard Color Setup
193: 
194: Define colors with terminal detection:
195: 
196: ```bash
197: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
198: # COLOR DEFINITIONS
199: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
200: 
201: RED='\033[0;31m'
202: GREEN='\033[0;32m'
203: YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
204: BLUE='\033[0;34m'
205: BOLD='\033[1m'
206: NC='\033[0m'  # No Color / Reset
207: 
208: # Disable colors if not terminal (for piping/logging)
209: if [[ ! -t 1 ]]; then
210:     RED='' GREEN='' YELLOW='' BLUE='' BOLD='' NC=''
211: fi
212: ```
213: 
214: **Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:13-22`
215: 
216: ### Usage in Output
217: 
218: ```bash
219: # Colored output
220: printf "${GREEN}SUCCESS:${NC} %s\n" "$message"
221: printf "${RED}ERROR:${NC} %s\n" "$error" >&2
222: printf "${YELLOW}WARNING:${NC} %s\n" "$warning"
223: ```
224: 
225: ---
226: 
227: ## 5. LOGGING FUNCTIONS
228: 
229: ### Standard Logging Pattern
230: 
231: ```bash
232: log_pass() {
233:     local rule="$1"
234:     local message="$2"
235:     printf "${GREEN}[PASS]${NC} ${BOLD}%s${NC}: %s\n" "$rule" "$message"
236: }
237: 
238: log_warn() {
239:     local rule="$1"
240:     local message="$2"

(Showing lines 1-240 of 623. Use offset=241 to continue.)
</content>

# Shell Style Guide

Coding conventions and formatting standards for Bash scripts in the OpenCode development environment.

---

## 1. OVERVIEW

### Purpose

Defines consistent styling rules for Bash scripts to ensure reliability, readability, and maintainability across all OpenCode shell scripts.

### Scope

Applies to all shell files in:
- `.opencode/skills/*/scripts/` - Skill automation scripts
- `.opencode/agents/scripts/` - Agent provider scripts
- `scripts/` - Build and deployment scripts

### Key Sources

| File | Evidence |
|------|----------|
| `lib/common.sh` | Color definitions, logging functions, utilities |
| `spec/create.sh` | Strict mode, argument parsing, control flow |

---

## 2. FILE STRUCTURE

### Shebang Line

**ALWAYS** use the portable bash shebang:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
```

**Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:1`

### File Header

Use the standard header format for identification:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# COMPONENT: [COMPONENT NAME]
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Brief description of what this script does.
#
# Usage: script.sh [options] <arguments>
```

**Alternative** (detailed header for major scripts):

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# SPECKIT: CREATE SPEC FOLDER
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Creates spec folder with templates based on documentation level.
#
# TEMPLATE ARCHITECTURE (v2.0 - CORE + ADDENDUM):
#   templates/
#   ├── level_1/        # Core only (~270 LOC total)
#   ├── level_2/        # Core + Verification (~390 LOC)
#   └── level_3/        # Core + Verification + Architecture (~540 LOC)
```

**Evidence**: `spec/create.sh:1-20`

### Strict Mode

**ALWAYS** enable strict mode immediately after the shebang/header:

```bash
set -euo pipefail
```

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `-e` | Exit on error (non-zero return) |
| `-u` | Error on undefined variables |
| `-o pipefail` | Pipe fails if any command fails |

**Evidence**: `spec/create.sh:22`

### Section Organization

Organize code into numbered sections:

```bash
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 1. COLOR DEFINITIONS
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

RED='\033[0;31m'
GREEN='\033[0;32m'
NC='\033[0m'

# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 2. UTILITY FUNCTIONS
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

log_info() {
    printf "${BLUE}INFO:${NC} %s\n" "$1"
}
```

**Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:9-35`

---

## 3. NAMING CONVENTIONS

### Functions

**Use**: `lowercase_with_underscores`

```bash
# Public functions
validate_input() {
    local input="$1"
    # ...
}

process_file() {
    local file_path="$1"
    # ...
}
```

### Private/Internal Functions

**Use**: Single underscore prefix for helpers:

```bash
# Internal helper function
_json_escape() {
    local str="$1"
    str="${str//\\/\\\\}"
    str="${str//\"/\\\"}"
    printf '%s' "$str"
}
```

**Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:37-45`

### Variables

**Use**: `lowercase_with_underscores` for local variables:

```bash
local file_path="$1"
local output_dir="${2:-./output}"
local is_valid=true
```

### Constants and Global Variables

**Use**: `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for constants and script-level variables:

```bash
readonly MAX_RETRIES=3
readonly DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=30

# Script-level state
ERRORS=0
WARNINGS=0
JSON_MODE=false
```

**Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:28-31`, `spec/create.sh:24-33`

---

## 4. COLOR DEFINITIONS

### Standard Color Setup

Define colors with terminal detection:

```bash
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# COLOR DEFINITIONS
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

RED='\033[0;31m'
GREEN='\033[0;32m'
YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
BLUE='\033[0;34m'
BOLD='\033[1m'
NC='\033[0m'  # No Color / Reset

# Disable colors if not terminal (for piping/logging)
if [[ ! -t 1 ]]; then
    RED='' GREEN='' YELLOW='' BLUE='' BOLD='' NC=''
fi
```

**Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:13-22`

### Usage in Output

```bash
# Colored output
printf "${GREEN}SUCCESS:${NC} %s\n" "$message"
printf "${RED}ERROR:${NC} %s\n" "$error" >&2
printf "${YELLOW}WARNING:${NC} %s\n" "$warning"
```

---

## 5. LOGGING FUNCTIONS

### Standard Logging Pattern

```bash
log_pass() {
    local rule="$1"
    local message="$2"
    printf "${GREEN}[PASS]${NC} ${BOLD}%s${NC}: %s\n" "$rule" "$message"
}

log_warn() {
    local rule="$1"
    local message="$2"

13: # Shell Quality Standards
14: 
15: Code quality requirements, validation rules, and best practices for Bash scripts in the OpenCode development environment.
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 1. OVERVIEW
20: 
21: ### Purpose
22: 
23: Establishes quality gates and validation criteria that all Bash scripts must meet before being committed or deployed.
24: 
25: ### Quality Tiers
26: 
27: | Tier | Requirement Level | Enforcement |
28: |------|-------------------|-------------|
29: | P0 | HARD BLOCKER | Must fix before commit |
30: | P1 | Required | Must fix or get approval |
31: | P2 | Recommended | Can defer with justification |

34:

36:

38: 
39: ### Shebang Line
40:

42: 
43: ```bash
44: #!/usr/bin/env bash
45: ```
46: 
47: **Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:1`
48: 
49: ### Strict Mode
50: 
51: Every script must enable strict mode:
52: 
53: ```bash
54: set -euo pipefail
55: ```
56: 
57: | Flag | Purpose | Why Required |
58: |------|---------|--------------|
59: | `-e` | Exit on error | Prevents silent failures |
60: | `-u` | Error on undefined vars | Catches typos, missing args |
61: | `-o pipefail` | Pipe failure propagation | Catches hidden pipe errors |
62: 
63: **Evidence**: `spec/create.sh:22`
64: 
65: ### Double-Quoted Variables
66: 
67: ALL variable expansions must be double-quoted:
68: 
69: ```bash
70: # Correct
71: echo "$variable"

80: 
81: **Exceptions**: Only inside `[[ ]]` arithmetic contexts:
82: ```bash

85: ```
86: 
87: ### File Header Present
88: 
89: Every script must have a header comment block:
90: 
91: ```bash

97: ```
98: 
99: ### No Commented-Out Code

102: 
103: ```bash

108: 
109: # OK - explanatory comment

111: ```
112: 
113: ### WHY Comments for Non-Obvious Code

121:

---
title: Shell Quality Standards
description: Code quality requirements, validation rules, and best practices for Bash scripts in the OpenCode development environment.

---

# Shell Quality Standards

Code quality requirements, validation rules, and best practices for Bash scripts in the OpenCode development environment.

---

## 1. OVERVIEW

### Purpose

Establishes quality gates and validation criteria that all Bash scripts must meet before being committed or deployed.

### Quality Tiers

| Tier | Requirement Level | Enforcement |
|------|-------------------|-------------|
| P0 | HARD BLOCKER | Must fix before commit |
| P1 | Required | Must fix or get approval |
| P2 | Recommended | Can defer with justification |

---

## 2. P0 - HARD BLOCKERS

These items MUST be fixed before any commit.

### Shebang Line

Every shell script must start with the portable shebang:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
```

**Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:1`

### Strict Mode

Every script must enable strict mode:

```bash
set -euo pipefail
```

| Flag | Purpose | Why Required |
|------|---------|--------------|
| `-e` | Exit on error | Prevents silent failures |
| `-u` | Error on undefined vars | Catches typos, missing args |
| `-o pipefail` | Pipe failure propagation | Catches hidden pipe errors |

**Evidence**: `spec/create.sh:22`

### Double-Quoted Variables

ALL variable expansions must be double-quoted:

```bash
# Correct
echo "$variable"
file_path="$1"
[[ -f "$file" ]]

# WRONG - WILL FAIL VALIDATION
echo $variable
file_path=$1
[[ -f $file ]]
```

**Exceptions**: Only inside `[[ ]]` arithmetic contexts:
```bash
# OK without quotes in arithmetic
if (( count > 10 )); then
```

### File Header Present

Every script must have a header comment block:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# COMPONENT: [COMPONENT NAME]
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Brief description of script purpose
```

### No Commented-Out Code

Remove all commented-out code. Use version control for history.

```bash
# WRONG - remove this
# old_function() {
#     echo "deprecated"
# }

# OK - explanatory comment
# Note: Using -f flag because...
```

### WHY Comments for Non-Obvious Code

Complex logic must include comments explaining WHY:

```bash
# WRONG - describes what (obvious)
# Loop through files
for f in *.md; do

# CORRECT - explains why
# Process only markdown files to avoid binary file corruption
for f in *.md; do
```

---

## 3. P1 - REQUIRED

These items must be addressed or receive explicit approval to defer.

### Function Naming

All functions must use `lowercase_with_underscores`:

```bash
# Correct
validate_input() { ... }
process_file() { ... }

# WRONG
validateInput() { ... }    # camelCase
ProcessFile() { ... }      # PascalCase
```

**Evidence**: `lib/common.sh:47-88`

### Local Variables in Functions

All function variables must be declared local:

```bash
# Correct
process_file() {
    local file_path="$1"
    local result=""
    # ...
}

# WRONG - pollutes global scope
process_file() {
    file_path="$1"
    result=""
}
```

### Exit Codes Documented

Non-trivial scripts must document exit codes:

```bash
# Exit Codes:
#   0 - Success
#   1 - Invalid arguments
#   2 - File not found
#   3 - Permission denied
```

### Error Output to stderr

All errors must go to stderr:

```bash
# Correct
echo "Error: file not found" >&2
printf "${RED}ERROR:${NC} %s\n" "$msg" >&2

# WRONG - error to stdout
echo "Error: file not found"
```

### TODOs With Context

TODOs must include owner or ticket:

```bash
# Correct
# TODO(username): Add input validation
# TODO(TICKET-123): Handle edge case

# WRONG
# TODO: fix this later
```

---

## 4. P2 - RECOMMENDED

These items improve quality but can be deferred.

### POSIX Portability

When possible, avoid bash-specific features for portability:

```bash
# More portable
command -v program >/dev/null 2>&1

# Bash-specific (but OK in OpenCode context)
type -t program >/dev/null 2>&1
```

### Consistent Color Scheme

Use the standard color definitions:

```bash
RED='\033[0;31m'
GREEN='\033[0;32m'
YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
BLUE='\033[0;34m'
BOLD='\033[1m'
NC='\033[0m'

if [[ ! -t 1 ]]; then
    RED='' GREEN='' YELLOW='' BLUE='' BOLD='' NC=''
fi
```

<content>
1: ---
2: title: TypeScript Style Guide
3: description: Formatting standards and naming conventions for TypeScript files in the OpenCode development environment.
4: trigger_phrases:
5:   - "opencode typescript style guide"
6:   - "typescript file header format"
7:   - "typescript formatting standards"
8:   - "typescript strict mode style"
9: importance_tier: normal
10: contextType: implementation
11: ---
12: 
13: # TypeScript Style Guide
14: 
15: Formatting standards and naming conventions for TypeScript files in the OpenCode development environment.
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 1. OVERVIEW
20: 
21: ### Purpose
22: 
23: Defines consistent styling rules for TypeScript files to ensure readability, maintainability, and alignment across all OpenCode TypeScript code.
24: 
25: ### When to Use
26: 
27: - Writing new TypeScript files
28: - Reviewing TypeScript code for consistency
29: - Resolving style disagreements in code review
30: 
31: ---
32: 
33: ## 2. FILE HEADER FORMAT
34: 
35: All TypeScript files MUST begin with a module header block identifying the module.
36: 
37: ### Template
38: 
39: ```typescript
40: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
41: // MODULE: [Module Name]
42: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
43: ```
44: 
45: ### Requirements
46: 
47: - Box width: 67 characters total (box-drawing line)
48: - Module name: Left-aligned within header block
49: - No `'use strict'` directive required (TypeScript `strict` mode in tsconfig replaces it)
50: - Immediately followed by imports
51: 
52: **Rationale**: TypeScript's `"strict": true` in `tsconfig.json` enables strict
53: type checking at compilation. Current system-spec-kit workspaces are
54: package-aware: `shared/` and `mcp_server/` use NodeNext ESM, `scripts/` uses
55: ES2022 ESM, and the root CommonJS setting is only a fallback default.
56: 
57: ### Full Header Example
58: 
59: ```typescript
60: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
61: // MODULE: Memory Search Handler
62: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
63: 
64: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
65: // 1. IMPORTS
66: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
67: 
68: import path from 'path';
69: import type { SearchOptions } from '../types';
70: ```
71: 
72: ---
73: 
74: ## 3. STRICT MODE
75: 
76: ### tsconfig.json Replaces 'use strict'
77: 
78: In JavaScript `.js/.cjs`, files require `'use strict';` at the top. In
79: TypeScript, the `tsconfig.json` setting `"strict": true` enforces type-system
80: strictness at the compiler level, while ESM workspaces are strict by runtime
81: semantics.
82: 
83: ```jsonc
84: // tsconfig.json
85: {
86:   "compilerOptions": {
87:     "strict": true  // Enables all strict type-checking options
88:   }
89: }
90: ```
91: 
92: **What `strict: true` enables:**
93: - `strictNullChecks` - null/undefined handled explicitly
94: - `strictFunctionTypes` - stricter function parameter checking
95: - `strictBindCallApply` - stricter bind/call/apply checking
96: - `strictPropertyInitialization` - class properties must be initialized
97: - `noImplicitAny` - parameters and variables must have types
98: - `noImplicitThis` - `this` must have explicit type
99: - `alwaysStrict` - parses source in strict mode and emits strict markers where the selected module target requires them
100: 
101: **Rule**: Do NOT add `'use strict';` to TypeScript source files. Use the module
102: format selected by the owning package (`nodenext`, `es2022`, or inherited
103: fallback).
104: 
105: ---
106: 
107: ## 4. SECTION ORGANIZATION
108: 
109: Large files are organized using numbered section dividers, consistent with JavaScript style.
110: 
111: ### Section Divider Templates
112: 
113: Two formats are acceptable. Use either consistently within a single file.
114: 
115: **Format A — Line-comment dividers** (used in most files):
116: 
117: ```typescript
118: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
119: // 1. [SECTION NAME]
120: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
121: ```
122: 
123: **Format B — Block-comment dividers** (used in 27+ files across the codebase):
124: 
125: ```typescript
126: /* ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
127:    1. SECTION NAME
128: ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
129: ```
130: 
131: Both formats serve the same purpose: visual separation of major code sections with numbered headings. Choose one format per file and apply it consistently.
132: 
133: ### Standard Section Order
134: 
135: | Order | Section Name      | Purpose                                |
136: |-------|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
137: | 1     | IMPORTS           | Module dependencies (with type imports)|
138: | 2     | TYPE DEFINITIONS  | Interfaces, types, enums               |
139: | 3     | CONSTANTS         | Configuration values, magic numbers    |
140: | 4     | HELPERS           | Internal utility functions              |
141: | 5     | CORE LOGIC        | Main implementation                    |
142: | 6     | EXPORTS           | Module public interface                |
143: 
144: **Key difference from JavaScript**: TypeScript files include a TYPE DEFINITIONS section (section 2) between imports and constants. This is where all interfaces, type aliases, and enums are defined.
145: 
146: Number sections from the first divider actually present in the file. File headers and module banners do not count, so a file whose first numbered divider is `TYPE DEFINITIONS` should label that divider `1`, not `2`.
147: 
148: ### TYPE DEFINITIONS Section Example (when IMPORTS is present as section 1)
149: 
150: ```typescript
151: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
152: // 2. TYPE DEFINITIONS
153: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
154: 
155: /** Configuration for search operations. */
156: interface SearchConfig {
157:   readonly maxResults: number;
158:   readonly timeout: number;
159:   readonly includeMetadata: boolean;
160: }
161: 
162: /** Possible states for a memory entry. */
163: type MemoryState = 'active' | 'archived' | 'pending';
164: 
165: /** Error codes for memory operations. */
166: enum ErrorCode {
167:   NotFound = 'NOT_FOUND',
168:   Timeout = 'TIMEOUT',
169:   ConnectionFailed = 'CONNECTION_FAILED',
170: }
171: ```
172: 
173: ---
174: 
175: ## 5. NAMING CONVENTIONS
176: 
177: ### Interface Names
178: 
179: **Style**: `PascalCase` (no `I` prefix for new interfaces)
180: 
181: ```typescript
182: // CORRECT — new interfaces
183: interface SearchResult { }
184: interface EmbeddingProvider { }
185: interface MemoryRecord { }
186: 
187: // CORRECT — legacy exception (documented below)
188: interface IEmbeddingProvider { }
189: interface IVectorStore { }
190: 
191: // INCORRECT
192: interface iSearchResult { }      // camelCase
193: interface search_result { }      // snake_case
194: interface ISearchResult { }      // I prefix on new interface
195: ```
196: 
197: **Legacy Exception**: `IEmbeddingProvider` and `IVectorStore` retain their `I` prefix for backward compatibility with re-export aliases across the codebase. All NEW interfaces omit the prefix. This exception is documented in the migration plan (Decision D5).
198: 
199: ### Type Alias Names
200: 
201: **Style**: `PascalCase`
202: 
203: ```typescript
204: // CORRECT
205: type SearchResult = { score: number; text: string };
206: type MemoryState = 'active' | 'archived' | 'pending';
207: type ScoreCalculator = (input: number) => number;
208: 
209: // INCORRECT
210: type searchResult = { };        // camelCase
211: type search_result = { };       // snake_case
212: ```
213: 
214: ### Enum Names and Members
215: 
216: **Style**: `PascalCase` name, `PascalCase` members
217: 
218: ```typescript
219: // CORRECT
220: enum ErrorCode {
221:   NotFound = 'NOT_FOUND',
222:   Timeout = 'TIMEOUT',
223:   ConnectionFailed = 'CONNECTION_FAILED',
224: }
225: 
226: enum MemoryTier {
227:   Constitutional = 'constitutional',
228:   Working = 'working',
229:   LongTerm = 'long_term',
230: }
231: 
232: // INCORRECT
233: enum errorCode { }              // camelCase name
234: enum ErrorCode { notFound }     // camelCase member
235: enum ERROR_CODE { }             // UPPER_SNAKE name
236: ```
237: 
238: ### Generic Type Parameters
239: 
240: **Style**: Single uppercase letter or `T`-prefixed descriptive name
241: 
242: ```typescript
243: // CORRECT — simple
244: function identity<T>(value: T): T { return value; }
245: 
246: // CORRECT — descriptive
247: function transform<TInput, TOutput>(input: TInput): TOutput { }
248: interface Repository<TEntity> { }
249: type Mapper<TSource, TTarget> = (source: TSource) => TTarget;
250: 
251: // INCORRECT
252: function identity<type>(value: type): type { }    // lowercase
253: function transform<input, output>(): void { }      // not T-prefixed
254: ```
255: 
256: ### Function Names
257: 
258: **Style**: `camelCase` (unchanged from JavaScript)
259: 
260: ```typescript

2: title: TypeScript Quality Standards
3: description: Type system patterns, error handling, documentation, async patterns, and tsconfig baseline for TypeScript files in the OpenCode development environment.
4: trigger_phrases:
5:   - "opencode typescript quality standards"

11: ---
12: 
13: # TypeScript Quality Standards
14: 
15: Type system patterns, error handling, documentation, async patterns, and tsconfig baseline for TypeScript files in the OpenCode development environment.
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 1. OVERVIEW
20: 
21: ### Purpose
22: 
23: Establishes type system patterns, error handling, documentation, async patterns, and tsconfig baseline that all TypeScript code in the OpenCode development environment must follow.
24: 
25: ### When to Use
26: 
27: - Writing new TypeScript modules
28: - Reviewing TypeScript code for quality compliance
29: - Setting up type safety, error handling, and documentation patterns
30: 
31: ---
32: 
33: ## 2. INTERFACE vs TYPE DECISION GUIDE
34: 
35: ### When to Use `interface`
36: 
37: Use `interface` for object shapes, class contracts, and data structures:
38: 
39: ```typescript
40: // Object shapes
41: interface SearchResult {
42:   readonly id: string;
43:   score: number;
44:   text: string;
45:   metadata: Record<string, unknown>;
46: }
47: 
48: // Class contracts
49: interface EmbeddingProvider {
50:   embed(text: string): Promise<number[]>;
51:   batchEmbed(texts: string[]): Promise<number[][]>;
52:   getDimension(): number;
53:   getModelName(): string;
54:   isReady(): boolean;
55: }
56: 
57: // Extending shapes
58: interface DetailedSearchResult extends SearchResult {
59:   highlights: string[];
60:   explanation: string;
61: }
62: ```
63: 
64: **Why interfaces for objects**: Interfaces support declaration merging, provide clearer error messages, and signal "this is a data contract" to the reader.
65: 
66: ### When to Use `type`
67: 
68: Use `type` for unions, intersections, mapped types, conditional types, and function signatures:
69: 
70: ```typescript
71: // Union types
72: type MemoryState = 'active' | 'archived' | 'pending' | 'deleted';
73: 
74: // Intersection types
75: type Timestamped<T> = T & { createdAt: number; updatedAt: number };
76: 
77: // Function signatures
78: type ScoreCalculator = (input: number, weight: number) => number;
79: 
80: // Conditional types
81: type AsyncReturn<T> = T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T;
82: 
83: // Mapped types
84: type Nullable<T> = { [K in keyof T]: T[K] | null };
85: 
86: // Tuple types
87: type Coordinates = [x: number, y: number];
88: ```
89: 
90: ### Decision Summary
91: 
92: | Use Case                           | Construct   | Reason                          |
93: |------------------------------------|-------------|---------------------------------|
94: | Object shapes / data structures    | `interface` | Extensible, clear error messages|
95: | Class contracts                    | `interface` | Supports `implements`           |
96: | Union types                        | `type`      | Only `type` supports unions     |
97: | Intersection / mapped / conditional| `type`      | Only `type` supports these      |
98: | Function signatures (standalone)   | `type`      | Cleaner syntax than interface    |
99: | Simple primitives / literals       | `type`      | `type ID = string`              |
100: 
101: ---
102: 
103: ## 3. TYPE SAFETY POLICIES
104: 
105: ### `unknown` Over `any`
106: 
107: **Policy**: Use `unknown` instead of `any` in public API surfaces. The `any` type disables type checking entirely. `unknown` forces callers to narrow the type before use.
108: 
109: ```typescript
110: // CORRECT — unknown forces type narrowing
111: function parseInput(raw: unknown): SearchConfig {
112:   if (typeof raw !== 'object' || raw === null) {
113:     throw new TypeError('Expected object input');
114:   }
115:   const obj = raw as Record<string, unknown>;
116:   // validate and narrow further...
117:   return obj as SearchConfig;
118: }
119: 
120: // INCORRECT — any disables all type checking
121: function parseInput(raw: any): SearchConfig {
122:   return raw;  // No validation, no safety
123: }
124: ```
125: 
126: **Permitted `any` usage** (must include justification comment):
127: 
128: ```typescript
129: // Interop with untyped third-party library (sqlite-vec native module)
130: // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any
131: const sqliteVec = require('sqlite-vec') as any;
132: ```
133: 
134: ### Strict Null Checks
135: 
136: With `strictNullChecks: true`, `null` and `undefined` must be handled explicitly:
137: 
138: ```typescript
139: // CORRECT — explicit null handling
140: function findMemory(id: string): MemoryRecord | null {
141:   const record = database.get(id);
142:   if (!record) return null;
143:   return record;
144: }
145: 
146: // Usage requires null check
147: const memory = findMemory('abc');
148: if (memory !== null) {
149:   console.log(memory.text);  // TypeScript knows memory is not null here
150: }
151: ```
152: 
153: ### Non-Null Assertions
154: 
155: **Policy**: Avoid non-null assertions (`!`). When absolutely necessary, include a justification comment:
156: 
157: ```typescript
158: // AVOID — non-null assertion hides potential bugs
159: const name = user!.name;
160: 
161: // PREFERRED — explicit null check
162: if (user === null) throw new Error('User not found');
163: const name = user.name;
164: 
165: // PERMITTED — with justification
166: // Process guarantees element exists after validation phase (see line 42)
167: const element = document.getElementById('root')!;
168: ```
169: 
170: ### Generic Constraints
171: 
172: Use `extends` to constrain generic type parameters:
173: 
174: ```typescript
175: // CORRECT — constrained generic
176: function getProperty<T extends Record<string, unknown>, K extends keyof T>(
177:   obj: T,
178:   key: K,
179: ): T[K] {
180:   return obj[key];
181: }
182: 
183: // CORRECT — constraint ensures required shape
184: interface HasId { readonly id: string; }
185: function findById<T extends HasId>(items: T[], id: string): T | undefined {
186:   return items.find(item => item.id === id);
187: }
188: 
189: // INCORRECT — unconstrained when constraint is needed
190: function getProperty<T>(obj: T, key: string): unknown {
191:   return (obj as Record<string, unknown>)[key];  // Loses type safety
192: }
193: ```
194: 
195: ---
196: 
197: ## 4. DISCRIMINATED UNIONS
198: 
199: Use discriminated unions for state management where an object can be in one of several distinct states:
200: 
201: ```typescript
202: // Discriminated union for operation results
203: interface SuccessResult<T> {
204:   readonly success: true;
205:   readonly data: T;
206: }
207: 
208: interface ErrorResult {
209:   readonly success: false;
210:   readonly error: string;
211:   readonly code: ErrorCode;
212: }
213: 
214: type OperationResult<T> = SuccessResult<T> | ErrorResult;
215: 
216: // Usage — TypeScript narrows automatically
217: function handleResult(result: OperationResult<SearchResult[]>): void {
218:   if (result.success) {
219:     // TypeScript knows: result.data exists
220:     console.log(`Found ${result.data.length} results`);

(Showing lines 1-220 of 824. Use offset=221 to continue.)
</content>

4: trigger_phrases:

12: 
13: # Code Organization - File Structure and Module Principles
14: 
15: File structure and module organization principles for OpenCode system code.
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 1. OVERVIEW
20: 
21: ### Purpose
22: 
23: This reference defines how to organize files, structure modules, and order imports across all languages in the OpenCode codebase. Consistent organization enables faster navigation and reduces cognitive load.
24: 
25: ### Core Principle
26: 
27: > **Predictable structure enables fast navigation.** Every file type has a known structure. Developers should find what they expect where they expect it.
28: 
29: ### When to Use
30: 
31: - Creating new files or modules
32: - Reorganizing existing code
33: - Deciding import order
34: - Reviewing code structure
35: 
36: ### Key Sources (Evidence)
37: 
38: | Pattern | Source File | Line Reference |
39: |---------|-------------|----------------|
40: | TypeScript module structure | `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` | Lines 1-70 |
41: | TypeScript exports | `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/core/config.ts` | Export block near file end |
42: | Python imports | `.opencode/skills/system-skill-advisor/mcp_server/scripts/skill_advisor.py` | Import block near file top |
43: | Shell structure | `lib/common.sh` | Lines 1-40 |
44: 
45: ---
46: 
47: ## 2. FILE STRUCTURE PRINCIPLES
48: 
49: ### Header-First Convention
50: 
51: Every file starts with a header block identifying its purpose:
52: 
53: **TypeScript / current ESM packages** (3-line module header):
54: ```javascript
55: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
56: // MODULE: Context Server
57: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
58: ```
59: 
60: **JavaScript `.js/.cjs` utilities** (boxed header plus strict mode):
61: ```javascript
62: // ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
63: // ║ COMPONENT: Example Utility                                              ║
64: // ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
65: 'use strict';
66: ```
67: 
68: **Python** (shebang + 3-line box):
69: ```python
70: #!/usr/bin/env python3
71: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72: # ADVISOR: SKILL ADVISOR
73: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
74: ```
75: 
76: **Shell** (shebang + header):
77: ```bash
78: #!/usr/bin/env bash
79: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
80: # LIBRARY: COMMON UTILITIES
81: # ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
82: ```
83: 
84: ### Module Type Labels
85: 
86: Use consistent labels in headers:
87: 
88: | Label | Use For |
89: |-------|---------|
90: | `SERVER` | MCP servers, HTTP servers |
91: | `HANDLER` | Request/event handlers |
92: | `LIBRARY`/`LIB` | Reusable utility modules |
93: | `SCRIPT` | Standalone executable scripts |
94: | `ADVISOR` | AI advisory/analysis tools |
95: | `VALIDATOR` | Validation/checking tools |
96: | `CONFIG` | Configuration files |
97: | `TEST` | Test files |
98: 
99: ### Standard File Sections
100: 
101: Every significant file should have numbered sections:
102: 
103: - Keep section titles uppercase and numbered (non-regression): `## 1. IMPORTS`, `## 2. CONSTANTS`.
104: - Do not count file headers, shebangs, module banners, docstrings, or strict-mode directives as numbered sections.
105: - Start at `1` for the first numbered code section that actually appears, then increment sequentially from the sections present in that file.
106: - Do not convert these headers to sentence case or unnumbered variants.
107: 
108: ```javascript
109: /* ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
110:    1. MODULE IMPORTS
111: ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
112: 
113: /* ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
114:    2. CONSTANTS
115: ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
116: 
117: /* ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
118:    3. HELPER FUNCTIONS
119: ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
120: 
121: /* ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
122:    4. MAIN LOGIC
123: ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
124: 
125: /* ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
126:    5. EXPORTS
127: ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
128: ```
129: 
130: ---
131: 
132: ## 3. MODULE ORGANIZATION
133: 
134: ### Single Responsibility
135: 
136: Each module should have ONE primary purpose:
137: 
138: **Good** - Single responsibility:
139: ```
140: mcp_server/
141: ├── handlers/
142: │   ├── memory-search.ts    # Handles memory search
143: │   ├── memory-save.ts      # Handles memory save
144: │   └── memory-crud.ts      # Handles memory CRUD
145: ├── lib/
146: │   ├── errors.ts           # Error definitions
147: │   ├── providers/
148: │   │   └── embeddings.ts   # Embedding generation
149: │   └── validation/
150: │       └── preflight.ts    # Input validation
151: ```
152: 
153: **Bad** - Mixed responsibilities:
154: ```
155: mcp_server/
156: ├── handlers.js             # All handlers (too large)
157: ├── utils.js                # "Utils" grab bag (unclear)
158: └── helpers.js              # More "helpers" (unclear)
159: ```
160: 
161: ### KISS/DRY/SOLID Structural Checks
162: 
163: Apply these checks during module organization:
164: 
165: 1. **KISS**: keep modules small and behavior-focused; avoid speculative abstraction layers.
166: 2. **DRY**: shared rules/constants live in one file and are imported by dependents.
167: 3. **SRP**: each module has one change reason.
168: 4. **OCP**: add variants through extension points before editing stable cores.
169: 5. **LSP/ISP/DIP**: avoid subtype surprises, oversized interfaces, and direct policy-to-infra coupling.
170: 
171: ### Directory Structure Pattern
172: 
173: ```
174: module/
175: ├── index.ts                # Entry point, exports public API
176: ├── core/                   # Core logic (private)
177: │   ├── config.ts
178: │   └── db-state.ts
179: ├── handlers/               # Request handlers
180: │   └── *.ts
181: ├── lib/                    # Utilities and helpers
182: │   ├── errors/
183: │   │   └── core.ts
184: │   ├── storage/
185: │   │   └── *.ts
186: │   └── utils/
187: │       └── *.ts
188: └── tests/                  # Test files
189:     └── *.test.ts
190: ```
191: 
192: ### Barrel Files (index.ts)
193: 
194: Use barrel files to expose public API:
195: 
196: ```typescript
197: // lib/errors/index.ts - Barrel file
198: export { MemoryError, ErrorCodes, buildErrorResponse } from './core';
199: export { getRecoveryHint, ERROR_CODES } from './recovery-hints';
200: export type { ErrorResponse, ErrorResponseData } from './core';
201: ```
202: 
203: Benefits:
204: - Single import point: `import { MemoryError } from './lib/errors';`
205: - Clear public API surface
206: - Implementation details hidden
207: 
208: ---
209: 
210: ## 4. IMPORT ORDERING
211: 
212: ### Universal Import Order
213: 
214: All languages follow this import order:
215: 
216: ```
217: 1. Standard library / Built-in modules
218: 2. Third-party packages (npm, pip, etc.)
219: 3. Local modules (project code)
220: 4. Type-only imports (TypeScript only — separate line, always last)

(Showing lines 1-220 of 699. Use offset=221 to continue.)
</content>
